Uthman later used her copy as the reference for his standardised Quran. The angel Jibrīl described her to the Prophet as a woman of prayer and fasting who would be his wife in Paradise. She narrated 60 hadiths, with six appearing in both Bukhari and Muslim.

school: Among the earliest converts to Islam despite her father Abu Sufyan being the foremost enemy of the Prophet; she migrated to Abyssinia where her husband Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh apostatised. The Negus of Abyssinia conducted her marriage to the Prophet on his behalf as a unique proxy ceremony across the sea. Her steadfast faith in isolation — divorced and abroad — is celebrated as a model of conviction. She narrated 65 hadiths and is ranked third in knowledge among the Mothers of the Believers.

school: Elder half-sister of Aisha and daughter of Abu Bakr; she earned her celebrated title by tearing her belt in two to secure provisions for the Prophet and her father hiding in Cave Thawr before the Hijra — doing so while seven months pregnant. One of the first 17-18 Muslims, she defied Abu Jahl's intimidation at her doorstep and later famously exhorted her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr to die with honour rather than surrender to al-Hajjaj. She died in 73 AH aged approximately 100, the last surviving Muhājir. Narrated 58 hadiths.

school: Among the earliest converts to Islam, Asma migrated twice — to Abyssinia with her first husband Jafar ibn Abi Talib, then to Medina. After Jafar's martyrdom at the Battle of Muta (629 CE) she successively married Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and later Ali ibn Abi Talib, making her the sole woman in Islamic history to have been wife to three of the four Rightly Guided Caliphs' closest circle. She was the confidante of Fatimah al-Zahra, washed her body at death per her final wishes, and was present at the most pivotal moments of the Prophetic era.

school: Anṣārī female companion who accompanied the Prophet on seven military campaigns, nursing the wounded and preparing food. The Prophet personally instructed her in the ritual washing of Zaynab bint Muhammad's body, and her transmission of this procedure became the definitive juristic source across all schools of Islamic law for the funeral bath of women. Al-Ṭabarānī preserved 85 of her traditions; six appear in both Bukhari and Muslim. She later taught in Basra, where Ali ibn Abi Talib visited her.

school: The most eloquent female companion of the Prophet; she famously approached the Prophet as delegate for all Muslim women to raise the question of spiritual equality and reward between men and women — a speech the Prophet praised as surpassing anything he had heard from a woman. She narrated 81 hadiths — the highest count among Anṣārī women. She accompanied the Conquest of Mecca and at the Battle of al-Yarmuk (636 CE) fought with a tent pole, reportedly killing nine Byzantine soldiers. She died in Damascus after a long life of teaching.

school: Born in Abyssinia during the first Muslim migration, step-daughter of the Prophet through his marriage to her mother Umm Salamah. The Prophet renamed her Zaynab and raised her in his household with great affection — reportedly splashing blessed water on her face in play, a gesture said to have preserved her youthful appearance into old age. She transmitted hadith from Umm Salamah, Aisha, Zaynab bint Jahsh, and Umm Habibah, forming a rare first-hand bridge to the inner household of the Prophet. Eight of her traditions are preserved by al-Ṭabarānī.

school: Female companion from the Custodians of the Kaaba (Banu Shayba) who narrated traditions directly from several of the Prophet's wives, forming a critical link in the hadith transmission network radiating from the Mothers of the Believers. Narrated from Aisha, Umm Salamah, Umm Habibah and Maymunah, as well as from the companion Asma bint Abi Bakr. Her son Abd al-Hamid ibn Jubayr and grandson Umayyah ibn Safwan were themselves transmitters who carried her traditions to the second generation.

school: Anṣārī female companion of the Prophet from Medina whose case with Thabit ibn Qays became one of the earliest and most cited precedents in Islamic jurisprudence for the khulʿ — the wife-initiated dissolution of marriage by returning the mahr. She came to the Prophet at dawn to request separation, a case recorded in canonical hadith collections. Her narrative contributed directly to the formation of Islamic family law on women's right to seek divorce.

A close neighbour of the Prophet, she and Amrah bint Abd al-Rahman both transmitted from her half-sister.

school: A child companion who performed the Farewell Pilgrimage with the Prophet Muhammad at the age of seven, an event he recorded in the canonical hadith of al-Bukhari (no. 1858) — 'I was taken to perform Hajj with the Messenger of Allah when I was seven years old.' As a child companion (min ṣighār al-ṣaḥāba) he lived long enough to transmit traditions to the Tabi'un and became a treasured living link to the Prophetic era; he also served as a market overseer (ṣāḥib al-sūq) in Medina under Umar ibn al-Khattab.

school: First wife of Prophet Muhammad and the very first person to embrace Islam; wealthy and respected Qurayshi merchant who proposed marriage to the Prophet in 595 CE. Her wealth, unwavering support and emotional strength were the bedrock of the early Muslim community. When the Prophet received the first revelation and was gripped with fear, it was Khadijah who reassured him, wrapped him in a cloak, and affirmed his prophethood. She financed the mission, ransomed persecuted Muslim slaves, and supported the community through the three-year economic boycott in the Shi'b Abi Talib. She bore him six children, including Fatimah al-Zahra. She died in 619 CE, the year the Prophet called 'Year of Sorrow'. Angel Jibril conveyed Allah's salaam to her. She is counted among the four greatest women of all time alongside Maryam, Asiya and Fatimah.

books.title: Khadijah, Daughter of Khuwaylid — Al-Islam.org
books.note: Detailed Arabic and English biography

school: Paternal uncle and foster brother of Prophet Muhammad; one of the most formidable warriors in early Islam. Converted to Islam in 616 CE after striking Abu Jahl who had insulted the Prophet, entering Dar al-Arqam immediately after. His conversion was a turning point that gave the early Muslim community courage and strength. He commanded the first Muslim military expedition. At the Battle of Badr (624 CE), he fought with two swords and was credited with decisive individual combat. At Uhud (625 CE), he was treacherously slain by Wahshi ibn Harb, an Abyssinian javelin-thrower hired by Hind bint Utba for revenge. His martyrdom devastated the Prophet, who wept and is narrated to have said that Jibril referred to him as 'the Lion of the Prophet' in the seven heavens. The Prophet called him 'Sayyid al-Shuhada'. His brave death is referenced in Surah al-Nahl (Q16:125-128).

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

school: Fourth person to accept Islam; the only companion whose name is explicitly mentioned in the Quran (Surah al-Ahzab 33:37). Originally from the Kalb tribe of Yemen, he was captured as a child and sold into slavery, purchased for Khadijah bint Khuwaylid who gave him as a gift to the Prophet. When his father came to ransom him, Zayd chose to remain with the Prophet, who then publicly adopted him at the Kaaba. He converted to Islam at the very beginning of the mission. The Prophet never sent any army without appointing Zayd as commander (Aisha's testimony). He participated in the Battle of Badr and commanded numerous expeditions. He was martyred at the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE) commanding a force of 3,000 Muslims against a 200,000-strong Byzantine army. The Prophet wept at his grave, calling him 'the yearning of the lover for the beloved'.

Cousin of Prophet Muhammad and elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib; among the earliest converts to Islam. Led the first migration (hijra) to Abyssinia in 616 CE, where he lived for twelve years. Served as the eloquent spokesman for the Muslim community before the Negus (King of Abyssinia), delivering one of the most celebrated early defenses of Islam, reciting the opening of Surah Maryam and moving the Christian king to tears. The Prophet rejoiced at his return from Abyssinia in 628 CE as much as at the conquest of Khaybar. Known for extraordinary generosity to the poor. Martyred at the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE) while commanding the army after Zayd ibn Harithah fell; he fought until both arms were severed, holding the banner against his chest. The Prophet reported that Jibril said Allah had given Jafar two wings to fly in Paradise in place of his severed arms.

Handsome, wealthy youth of the Quraysh who embraced Islam in 614 CE in the house of al-Arqam and endured severe family persecution for his faith. After emigrating to Abyssinia, he was chosen by the Prophet as Islam's first diplomatic envoy — sent to Yathrib (Madinah) after the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE) to teach Quran, prepare the city for the Hijra, and unite the Aws and Khazraj tribes. His mission was transformative: within a year, senior tribal chiefs including Sa'd ibn Muadh and Usayd ibn Hudayr embraced Islam, leading to mass conversions. He also led the first Friday Jumu'ah prayer ever performed in Madinah. He carried the Muslim flag at the Battle of Badr and at Uhud, where he was martyred — dying so poor he was shrouded in a single patched cloth barely long enough to cover him fully.

Chief of the Banu Abd al-Ashhal clan of the Aws tribe in Madinah; one of the most pivotal Ansar companions. Converted to Islam in 622 CE (1 AH) through Musab ibn Umayr, whereupon his entire subtribe of Banu Abd al-Ashhal — the first household in Madinah — converted en masse, breaking their idols. His conversion and leadership were critical to the social foundation of the Islamic state in Madinah. He participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, and the Trench. At Badr he was the standard-bearer of the Aws. At the Battle of the Trench (627 CE), he was struck by an arrow that severed a main vein; he lived long enough to deliver his famous verdict against the treacherous Banu Qurayza, which the Prophet declared was 'the judgment of Allah'. He died of his wounds at age 37. The Prophet stood barefoot at his funeral and declared 'The Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the death of Sa'd ibn Muadh.' Narrated that 70,000 angels attended his funeral prayer.

Elder half-brother of Umar ibn al-Khattab; converted to Islam before Umar, preceding him in this distinction. Among the earliest Muhajirun who emigrated to Madinah; the Prophet established a bond of brotherhood between him and Ma'n ibn Adi. Participated in the Battle of Badr, Uhud, Khandaq and all campaigns with the Prophet, refusing armour at Uhud so as to be equal with his brother in seeking martyrdom. After the Prophet's death (632 CE), he served as deputy commander under Khalid ibn al-Walid in the Ridda Wars. At the Battle of Yamamah against the false prophet Musaylimah, Zayd served as standard-bearer. When Muslims wavered, he rallied them with a stirring speech, fought until slain, still holding the banner. Umar mourned deeply, saying 'My brother surpassed me in two virtues: he embraced Islam before me and was martyred before me.' His martyrdom alongside many Quran memorisers prompted Umar to urge Abu Bakr to commission the first collection of the Quran.

Persian-born freed slave adopted by Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utba of the Quraysh. Among the earliest Muslim converts; one of four primary Quran teachers personally recommended by the Prophet. The Prophet said: 'Learn the Quran from four persons: Abdullah ibn Masud, Salim Mawla Abi Hudhayfah, Ubayy ibn Kab and Muadh ibn Jabal.' Aisha heard his beautiful Quran recitation one night; the Prophet came to listen and praised Allah for placing someone like Salim in the Ummah. Participated in Badr and all major battles. At the Battle of Yamamah (632 CE) he held the Muslim banner until his right hand was severed, then held it with his left, reciting Quran until martyrdom. Umar, dying years later, said 'If Salim were alive, I would have appointed him my successor.' His death was one of the chief motivations for compiling the Quran into a single written volume.

Noble Qurayshi companion from Banu Abd Shams; son of the leading Quraysh chieftain Utba ibn Rabi'a. Embraced Islam before the Prophet entered Dar al-Arqam — among the very earliest Muslims. Participated in both migrations to Abyssinia where his son Muhammad was born. Participated in the Battle of Badr where he faced the tragic position of his own father fighting against the Muslims. Fought at Uhud, Khandaq and all major engagements. He adopted Salim, the freed Persian slave who became one of the great Quran teachers. At the Battle of Yamamah (632 CE), he and Salim embraced, made a vow of martyrdom together, shouted 'O People of the Quran!' to rally the Muslims, and both were found slain side by side — one's head at the feet of the other.

Considered the seventh person to embrace Islam. Participated in both migrations to Abyssinia. Tall and impressive, known for exceptional skill with spear and arrow; participated in Badr, Uhud, Khandaq, Yamamah and many campaigns. Under Caliph Umar, he commanded 2,000 men against the Persian garrison at Ubullah (Iraq) in 635 CE, opening lower Mesopotamia to Islam. He founded the garrison city of Basra — one of the great cities of the Islamic world — planning it strategically between the Arabian Desert and the Persian Gulf, building its first mosque. Concerned that Muslims were softening to worldly comforts, he delivered a famous sermon on zuhd (detachment): recalling early Islam when he and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas shared a single cloak. He asked Umar to relieve him of governorship; Umar refused, and on his return journey to Basra he fell from his camel and died.

Ansari companion from the Aws tribe, Banu Haritha clan; an early Medinan convert. Participated in the Battle of Badr, Uhud and Khandaq. Renowned as the Prophet's most trusted special envoy and special operations commander: assigned delicate, sensitive missions including leadership of the mission against Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf who had incited war against the Muslims with his poetry, as well as numerous other expeditions. He held himself completely apart from the first Fitna (civil war) after Uthman's murder, refusing absolutely to fight any Muslim regardless of faction, maintaining strict neutrality — breaking his sword to symbolise this — a principled stance that distinguished him from virtually all other companions. He died at approximately age 70 in Madinah during the caliphate of Muawiya, one of the last of the Badr companions to die.

One of the Asharah Mubasharah — the Ten Companions explicitly promised Paradise by the Prophet ﷺ. Among the earliest Muslims, converting before the Prophet entered Dar al-Arqam; one of the eight who first accepted Islam. At the Battle of Uhud (625 CE), he shielded the Prophet with his own body when archers broke rank; he sustained 24 wounds and lost the use of two fingers, but kept the Prophet from falling. The Prophet later said of this day: 'Whoever wishes to see a martyr walking the earth, let him look at Talha ibn Ubaydullah.' Known throughout the Muslim community as 'Al-Fayyadh' (the Bountiful) for his extraordinary generosity — he once distributed 700,000 dirhams in a single day to those in need. He fought at Badr, Uhud, and all major campaigns. He was martyred at the Battle of the Camel (36 AH / 656 CE) in Basra, caught in the civil conflict over the caliphate. He is buried in Basra.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

school: The first diplomat in Islamic history and one of the most remarkable transformation stories among the companions. Born to one of the wealthiest Quraysh families, he was famous for his beauty, fine clothes, and luxury — yet he abandoned everything for Islam, converting secretly around 614 CE in the house of al-Arqam. When his family discovered his conversion, they imprisoned and tortured him. He emigrated to Abyssinia. After the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the Prophet appointed him as Islam's first official envoy to Madinah — charged with teaching Quran, uniting the fractious Aws and Khazraj tribes, and preparing the city for the Prophet's arrival. Within a year, his mission had transformed Madinah: senior tribal chiefs including Sa'd ibn Muadh embraced Islam, and mass conversions followed. He led the first Friday Jumu'ah prayer in Madinah. He carried the Muslim standard at Badr and Uhud, where he was martyred while defending the Prophet. He died so poor that there was only one small cloth to cover him — when placed over his head, his feet showed; when placed over his feet, his head showed. The Prophet wept. He is buried on the battlefield of Uhud.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

school: Paternal uncle of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, approximately two or three years older than his nephew. He was sympathetic to the Prophet's mission from the beginning but publicly accepted Islam late — some accounts say he was already Muslim secretly but maintained his position among the Quraysh to protect Muslims. He was captured at the Battle of Badr fighting on the Quraysh side; the Prophet freed him after he paid a large ransom. He formally embraced Islam at the time of the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE / 8 AH) and thereafter distinguished himself. He participated at the Battle of Hunayn and gave a pivotal rallying call when the Muslims began to flee, shouting out to the companions — his powerful voice turning the rout into a renewed charge. After the Prophet's death, he was one of the most respected figures in the community. He died at age approximately 88 in Madinah. The Abbasid Caliphate (750–1258 CE), which ruled the Islamic world for five centuries, descended from him.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

school: Elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and son of Abu Talib. He was approximately twenty years older than Ali. Known as the greatest genealogist of the Quraysh — the Prophet frequently consulted him on matters of tribal lineage. He embraced Islam after the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE). He participated in the Battle of Hunayn and subsequent campaigns. He was blind in old age and lived during the caliphate of Ali, but maintained a complex relationship with his brother's rule, eventually residing at Muawiyah's court in Damascus due to financial difficulties. He narrated hadith and is a transmitter in various chains. His descendants include many important figures in later Islamic history. He died during the caliphate of Muawiyah (c. 670 CE).

school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ present at the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay'at al-Ridwan) at Hudaybiyya in 628 CE / 6 AH. school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ present at the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay'at al-Ridwan) at Hudaybiyya in 628 CE / 6 AH. Allah honoured all who were present by name in the Quran: 'Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts' (Q48:18). Being of Ridwan is one of the highest designations a companion could hold. Participated in: Khandaq. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

school: Poet-Companion (Sha'ir al-Rasul) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Poetry was the most powerful medium of public opinion in 7th-century Arabia, and these companions used their talent in defence of Islam, celebrating its victories, mourning its martyrs, and refuting the hostile verse of the Quraysh. The Prophet encouraged them, saying: 'Respond on our behalf — may Jibril support you.' Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who was martyred at Hijaz, Uhud. The seventy martyrs of Uhud hold a special place in Islamic memory — the Prophet himself wept over them and they are buried collectively on the battlefield. The Prophet said: 'I am a witness over them.' Died as a martyr (625 CE) at Hijaz, Uhud.

Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

school: Among the first judges (Qudah) appointed in Islam, serving as a Qadhi (judge) during the early Islamic period. Islamic jurisprudence grew out of the practice of these first companions who ruled on disputes according to the Quran and the Prophet's practice, establishing the precedents that later became formal law. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.
